sanskrit: प्रणम्य शिरसा विष्णुं त्रैलोक्याधिपतिं प्रभुम् । नानाशास्त्रोद्धृतं वक्ष्ये राजनीतिसमुच्चयम् ॥
transliteration: praṇamya śirasā viṣṇuṁ trailokya-ādhipatiṁ prabhum | nānā-śāstroddhṛtaṁ vakṣye rājanīti-samuccayam ||
translation: Bowing my head before the Lord Vishnu, master of the three worlds, I shall speak this compilation of political wisdom extracted from various scriptures.
meaning: Chanakya begins by paying homage to Vishnu and declares his intent to compile practical wisdom from various sources.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अधीत्य चतुरो वेदान्सर्वशास्त्राण्यनेकशः । ब्रह्मतत्त्वं न जानाति दर्वी पाकरसं यथा ॥
transliteration: adhītya caturo vedān sarva-śāstrāṇy anekaśaḥ | brahma-tattvaṁ na jānāti darvī pāka-rasaṁ yathā ||
translation: One may study the four Vedas and all the scriptures many times over, but like a ladle that does not know the taste of the dish it stirs, one who has not realized Brahman knows nothing.
meaning: Bookish knowledge without inner realization is hollow — like a ladle that stirs a delicious dish but cannot taste it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: दुर्जनः परिहर्तव्यो विद्यया अलंकृतोपि सन् । मणिना भूषितः सर्पः किमसौ न भयंकरः ॥
transliteration: durjanaḥ parihartavyo vidyayā alaṁkṛto'pi san | maṇinā bhūṣitaḥ sarpaḥ kim asau na bhayaṁkaraḥ ||
translation: Even if a wicked person is adorned with learning, he should be avoided. Is a snake adorned with a gem any less dangerous?
meaning: An educated but evil person is still dangerous — learning alone does not reform a corrupt character.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: न विश्वसेत्कुमित्रे च मित्रे चापि न विश्वसेत् । कदाचित्कुपितं मित्रं सर्वगुह्यं प्रकाशयेत् ॥
transliteration: na viśvaset kumittre ca mitre cāpi na viśvaset | kadācit kupitaṁ mitraṁ sarva-guhyaṁ prakāśayet ||
translation: Do not trust a bad friend; do not trust even a good friend too blindly. If the friend becomes angry someday, he may reveal all your secrets.
meaning: Be cautious even with good friends — in anger, trusted people can expose your deepest secrets.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: मनसा चिन्तितं कार्यं वाचा नैव प्रकाशयेत् । मन्त्रेण रक्षयेद्गूढं कार्ये चापि नियोजयेत् ॥
transliteration: manasā cintitaṁ kāryaṁ vācā naiva prakāśayet | mantreṇa rakṣayed gūḍhaṁ kārye cāpi niyojayet ||
translation: Whatever work you plan in your mind, do not reveal it with your words. Keep it guarded like a mantra and execute it with action.
meaning: Wise action requires keeping plans secret — reveal through action, not speech.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अनालोचितकार्याणां भवत्यर्थस्य संक्षयः । लक्ष्मीर्भवति दानेन न तु संग्रहणादपि ॥
transliteration: anālocita-kāryāṇāṁ bhavaty arthasya saṁkṣayaḥ | lakṣmīr bhavati dānena na tu saṁgrahaṇād api ||
translation: Acting without forethought leads to the ruin of one's wealth. Wealth grows through giving, not by hoarding.
meaning: Thoughtless action destroys resources. True prosperity comes through generosity, not miserliness.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: को हि भारः समर्थानाम् किं दूरं व्यवसायिनाम् । को विदेशः सुविद्यानाम् कः परः प्रियवादिनाम् ॥
transliteration: ko hi bhāraḥ samarthānāṁ kiṁ dūraṁ vyavasāyinām | ko videśaḥ suvidhyānāṁ kaḥ paraḥ priyavādinām ||
translation: What burden is too heavy for the capable? What place is too far for the determined? What land is foreign to the learned? Who is a stranger to one who speaks sweetly?
meaning: Ability conquers difficulty, determination conquers distance, learning conquers alienation, and kindness conquers separation.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एकाकी चिन्तयेद्धर्मं द्वाभ्यामध्यायनं चरेत् । त्रिभिर्गायेत्पदान्यर्थान् चतुर्भिः सह धावति ॥
transliteration: ekākī cintayed dharmaṁ dvābhyām adhyāyanaṁ caret | tribhir gāyet padāny arthān caturbhiḥ saha dhāvati ||
translation: Think about dharma alone; study in pairs; sing hymns in threes; and travel in groups of four.
meaning: This verse offers practical wisdom for companionship in different activities — solitude for reflection, small groups for study.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आयुः कर्म च वित्तं च विद्या निधनमेव च । पञ्चैतानि हि सृज्यन्ते गर्भस्थस्यैव देहिनः ॥
transliteration: āyuḥ karma ca vittaṁ ca vidyā nidhanam eva ca | pañcaitāni hi sṛjyante garbhasthasyaiva dehinaḥ ||
translation: Lifespan, vocation, wealth, learning, and death — these five are determined for a being while still in the womb.
meaning: Certain fundamentals of life are predestined — but this does not excuse laziness; it means one should act with wisdom within one's destiny.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: साधुभ्यस्ते निवर्तन्ते पुत्राः मित्राणि बान्धवाः । ये च तैः सह गन्तारस्तद्धर्मात्सुकृतं कुलम् ॥
transliteration: sādhubhyas te nivartante putrāḥ mitrāṇi bāndhavāḥ | ye ca taiḥ saha gantāras tad dharmāt sukṛtaṁ kulam ||
translation: Sons, friends, and relatives who are devoted to virtuous people — in that family dharma flourishes.
meaning: The character of a family is reflected in the virtue of its members and the company they keep.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अनृतं साहसं माया मूर्खत्वमतिलोभता । अशौचत्वं निर्दयत्वं स्त्रीणां दोषाः स्वभावजाः ॥
transliteration: anṛtaṁ sāhasaṁ māyā mūrkhatvam ati-lobhatā | aśaucatvaṁ nirdayatvaṁ strīṇāṁ doṣāḥ svabhāvajāḥ ||
translation: Falsehood, rashness, deception, foolishness, excessive greed, impurity, and cruelty — these are natural faults of women.
meaning: This is a classical critique from Chanakya's era; it should be read as a comment on certain tendencies, not as absolute truth about all women.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: भोजनं ह्याशु पच्यते भर्त्रा हि सह शेरते । परपुंसि च सज्जन्ते प्राज्ञाः स्त्रीषु न विश्वसेत् ॥
transliteration: bhojanaṁ hy āśu pacyate bhartrā hi saha śerate | para-puṁsi ca sajjante prājñāḥ strīṣu na viśvaset ||
translation: The learned do not blindly trust women, for they digest food quickly, share beds with husbands, and also attach to other men.
meaning: Chanakya counsels caution in relationships; his view reflects the political cynicism of his age.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: न विश्वसेदविश्वस्ते विश्वस्ते नातिविश्वसेत् । विश्वासाद्भयमुत्पन्नं मूलान्यपि निकृन्तति ॥
transliteration: na viśvased aviśvaste viśvaste nāti-viśvaset | viśvāsād bhayam utpannaṁ mūlāny api nikṛntati ||
translation: Do not trust the untrustworthy; do not trust even the trustworthy too completely. Danger arising from trust cuts at the roots.
meaning: Blind trust is dangerous — even reliable people can become a source of harm under certain circumstances.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: कष्टं च खलु मूर्खत्वं कष्टं च खलु यौवनम् । कष्टादपि कष्टतरं परगेहे निवासनम् ॥
transliteration: kaṣṭaṁ ca khalu mūrkhatvaṁ kaṣṭaṁ ca khalu yauvanam | kaṣṭād api kaṣṭataraṁ para-gehe nivāsanam ||
translation: Foolishness is painful; youth is painful; but more painful than both is living in another's house.
meaning: Dependence on others is the greatest source of misery — self-reliance is paramount.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: नाग्निर्हरति नो राजा नो भ्राता नो पिता सुहृत् । नावयोः सहयात्रास्ति धर्मो हरति केवलम् ॥
transliteration: nāgnir harati no rājā no bhrātā no pitā suhṛt | nāvayoḥ saha-yātrāsti dharmo harati kevalam ||
translation: Neither fire, king, brother, father, nor friend will accompany you in death — only dharma travels with you.
meaning: Only the virtue (dharma) you have cultivated travels with the soul after death; all worldly companions are left behind.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: जानीयात्प्रेषणे भृत्यान् बान्धवान् व्यसनाभरे । मित्रं चापत्काले तथा भार्यां च विभवक्षये ॥
transliteration: jānīyāt preṣaṇe bhṛtyān bāndhavān vyasanābhare | mitraṁ cāpat-kāle tathā bhāryāṁ ca vibhava-kṣaye ||
translation: Test servants in discharge of duties, relatives in difficulties, a friend in adversity, and a wife when wealth is gone.
meaning: True character reveals itself only under pressure — adversity is the best test of loyalty.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आतुरे व्यसने प्राप्ते दुर्भिक्षे शत्रुसंकटे । राजद्वारे श्मशाने च यस्तिष्ठति स बान्धवः ॥
transliteration: āture vyasane prāpte durbhikṣe śatru-saṁkaṭe | rāja-dvāre śmaśāne ca yas tiṣṭhati sa bāndhavaḥ ||
translation: One who stands by you in sickness, misfortune, famine, enemy attack, at the royal court, and at the cremation ground — that person is truly your relative.
meaning: True family are those who remain through the hardest circumstances — illness, poverty, danger, and death.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यस्य पुत्रो वशीभूतो भार्या छन्दानुगामिनी । विभवे यस्य सन्तोषस्तस्य स्वर्ग इहैव हि ॥
transliteration: yasya putro vaśī-bhūto bhāryā chandānugāminī | vibhave yasya santoṣas tasya svarga ihaiva hi ||
translation: He whose son is obedient, whose wife follows his wishes, and who is content with what he has — for him, heaven is right here on earth.
meaning: A harmonious family and a contented mind make earth itself into paradise.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एकं वित्तं व्ययेदर्थे द्वाभ्यां कार्यमनुष्ठितम् । त्रिभिर्गतं न चोद्वेगी चतुर्भिः प्राप्यते फलम् ॥
transliteration: ekaṁ vittaṁ vyayed arthe dvābhyāṁ kāryam anuṣṭhitam | tribhir gataṁ na codvegī caturbhiḥ prāpyate phalam ||
translation: One should sacrifice money for a worthy cause, wealth should be used in service, three-quarters gone should not cause grief, and by all four means one attains the fruit.
meaning: Money is a means, not an end — use it purposefully for dharma, and do not mourn its expenditure.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: तावद्भयेषु भेतव्यं यावद्भयमनागतम् । आगतं तु भयं वीक्ष्य प्रहर्तव्यमशङ्कया ॥
transliteration: tāvad bhayeṣu bhetavyaṁ yāvad bhayam anāgatam | āgataṁ tu bhayaṁ vīkṣya prahartavyam aśaṅkayā ||
translation: Fear the danger while it is still distant; when danger arrives, strike it fearlessly.
meaning: Prudence demands foresight in planning against danger; but when danger comes, act without hesitation or fear.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: कस्य दोषः कुले नास्ति व्याधिना को न पीडितः । व्यसनं केन न प्राप्तं कस्य सौख्यं निरन्तरम् ॥
transliteration: kasya doṣaḥ kule nāsti vyādhinā ko na pīḍitaḥ | vyasanaṁ kena na prāptaṁ kasya saukhyaṁ nirantaram ||
translation: Whose family is without flaw? Who is never afflicted by disease? Who has never faced misfortune? Whose happiness is uninterrupted?
meaning: No one is free from hardship, family flaws, illness, or misfortune — suffering is universal.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आचारः कुलमाख्याति देशमाख्याति भाषणम् । सम्भ्रमः स्नेहमाख्याति वपुराख्याति भोजनम् ॥
transliteration: ācāraḥ kulam ākhyāti deśam ākhyāti bhāṣaṇam | sambhramaḥ sneham ākhyāti vapur ākhyāti bhojanam ||
translation: One's conduct reveals one's family; speech reveals one's region; reverence reveals affection; and the body reveals one's diet.
meaning: A person's background, character, relationships, and health are all revealed through observable signs.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सुकुले योजयेत्कन्यां पुत्रं विद्यासु योजयेत् । व्यसने योजयेच्छत्रुं मित्रं धर्मेषु योजयेत् ॥
transliteration: sukule yojayet kanyāṁ putraṁ vidyāsu yojayet | vyasane yojayet śatrum mitraṁ dharmeṣu yojayet ||
translation: Give your daughter to a good family; engage your son in studies; engage the enemy in vice; and engage your friend in dharma.
meaning: Wise management of relationships: marry daughters well, educate sons, lead enemies toward self-destruction, and guide friends toward virtue.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: दुर्गं जह्यात्सुहृद्युक्तः शत्रोर्दुर्गम् विलापयेत् । अज्ञातानन्वयं ज्ञात्वा परबुद्धिं प्रतीक्षते ॥
transliteration: durgaṁ jahyāt suhṛd-yuktaḥ śatror durgam vilāpayet | ajñātān anvayaṁ jñātvā para-buddhiṁ pratīkṣate ||
translation: Abandon a fortress with the help of allies; destroy the enemy's fortress; know the unknown through lineage; and wait to understand the opponent's mind.
meaning: Strategic thinking in statecraft: know when to yield, when to attack, and when to wait and observe.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: वृद्धाः सेव्या विशेषेण विप्राः शास्त्रविशारदाः । सदाचारास्त्वयं लोको जिताः स्युर्गुरुभिः स्वयम् ॥
transliteration: vṛddhāḥ sevyā viśeṣeṇa viprāḥ śāstra-viśāradāḥ | sadācārās tv ayaṁ loko jitāḥ syur gurubhiḥ svayam ||
translation: Especially serve the elders and learned ones who are expert in scriptures. Those of good conduct conquer the world through their teachers.
meaning: Service to learned elders and virtuous teachers is the path to worldly and spiritual success.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: विप्रो वृक्षस्तस्य मूलं संध्योपासनमेव च । स्कन्धो वेदश्च शाखाश्च धर्मकार्याणि सर्वशः ॥
transliteration: vipro vṛkṣas tasya mūlaṁ sandhyopāsanam eva ca | skandho vedaś ca śākhāś ca dharma-kāryāṇi sarvaśaḥ ||
translation: A Brahmin is like a tree: the root is his morning and evening prayers, the trunk is the Vedas, and the branches are all his actions of dharma.
meaning: Spiritual practice is the foundation from which all virtuous action grows — remove the roots and the whole tree falls.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: माता शत्रुः पिता वैरी येन बालो न पाठितः । न शोभते सभामध्ये हंसमध्ये बको यथा ॥
transliteration: mātā śatruḥ pitā vairi yena bālo na pāṭhitaḥ | na śobhate sabhā-madhye haṁsa-madhye bako yathā ||
translation: A mother is an enemy and a father is a foe who does not educate their child. The uneducated child shines not in an assembly, like a crane among swans.
meaning: Parents who fail to educate their children are doing them harm — education is the greatest gift to a child.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: लालनाद्बहवो दोषास्ताडनाद्बहवो गुणाः । तस्मात्पुत्रं च शिष्यं च ताडयेन्न तु लालयेत् ॥
transliteration: lālanād bahavo doṣās tāḍanād bahavo guṇāḥ | tasmāt putraṁ ca śiṣyaṁ ca tāḍayen na tu lālayet ||
translation: Excessive indulgence creates many faults; discipline creates many virtues. Therefore, sons and disciples should be disciplined, not over-indulged.
meaning: Firm discipline in education and upbringing develops character; indulgence spoils it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: श्लोकेन वा द्वाभ्यां वापि श्लोकार्धेन त्रिभिरपि। वाक्यं वा वाक्यार्धमर्थं वा ग्राह्यमश्नुते नरः ॥
transliteration: ślokena vā dvābhyāṁ vāpi ślokārdhena tribhir api | vākyaṁ vā vākyārdham arthaṁ vā grāhyam aśnute naraḥ ||
translation: A person should absorb the essence of knowledge — whether from one verse, two, or even half a verse, one should extract its meaning.
meaning: The quality of learning matters more than quantity — even half a verse understood deeply is worth more than hundreds recited by rote.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: दातव्यमिति यद्दानं दीयतेऽनुपकारिणे । देशे काले च पात्रे च तद्दानं सात्त्विकं स्मृतम् ॥
transliteration: dātavyam iti yad dānaṁ dīyate'nupakāriṇe | deśe kāle ca pātre ca tad dānaṁ sāttvikaṁ smṛtam ||
translation: Charity given out of a sense of duty, to one who does not return the favor, at the right place, time, and to the right person — that is called sattvic charity.
meaning: True charity is given without expectation of return, at the appropriate time and place, to a deserving recipient.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: गुणैरुत्तमतां याति नोच्चैरासनसंस्थितः । प्रासादशिखरस्थोऽपि काकः किं गरुडायते ॥
transliteration: guṇair uttamatāṁ yāti noccair-āsana-saṁsthitaḥ | prāsāda-śikhara-stho'pi kākaḥ kiṁ garuḍāyate ||
translation: A person attains greatness through virtues, not by sitting in a high seat. A crow sitting on the roof of a palace — does it become an eagle?
meaning: Position or power does not confer greatness; only inner virtue and genuine merit do.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यस्य नास्ति स्वयं प्रज्ञा शास्त्रं तस्य करोति किम् । लोचनाभ्यां विहीनस्य दर्पणः किं करिष्यति ॥
transliteration: yasya nāsti svayaṁ prajñā śāstraṁ tasya karoti kim | locanābhyāṁ vihīnasya darpaṇaḥ kiṁ kariṣyati ||
translation: What can the scriptures do for one who lacks personal wisdom? Of what use is a mirror to one who is blind?
meaning: Learning is useless without personal wisdom and judgment — scriptures are a tool, not a substitute for thinking.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: दूरस्थोऽपि न दूरस्थो यो यस्य मनसि स्थितः । यो यस्य हृदये नास्ति समीपस्थोऽपि दूरतः ॥
transliteration: dūrastho'pi na dūra-stho yo yasya manasi sthitaḥ | yo yasya hṛdaye nāsti samīpastho'pi dūrataḥ ||
translation: One who dwells in someone's mind is not truly far away. One who is not in the heart is far away, even if physically near.
meaning: True closeness is emotional and mental, not physical — presence in the heart is the only real nearness.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यथा ह्येकेन चक्रेण न रथस्य गतिर्भवेत् । एवं पुरुषकारेण विना दैवं न सिध्यति ॥
transliteration: yathā hy ekena cakreṇa na rathasya gatir bhavet | evaṁ puruṣakāreṇa vinā daivaṁ na sidhyati ||
translation: Just as a chariot cannot move on one wheel, so destiny cannot be fulfilled without human effort.
meaning: Fate and effort must work together — destiny alone, without action, cannot produce results.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: विषादप्यमृतं ग्राह्यमेध्यादपि कनकम् । अमित्रादपि सद्वृत्तं बालादपि सुभाषितम् ॥
transliteration: viṣād apy amṛtaṁ grāhyam edhyād api kanakam | amitrād api sad-vṛttaṁ bālād api subhāṣitam ||
translation: Take nectar even from poison; take gold even from filth; take good conduct even from an enemy; take good words even from a child.
meaning: Wisdom has no fixed source — accept it wherever it appears, regardless of the vessel.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: क्षमावशीकृतिर्लोके क्षमया किं न साध्यते । शान्तिखड्गः करे यस्य किं करिष्यति दुर्जनः ॥
transliteration: kṣamā vaśīkṛtir loke kṣamayā kiṁ na sādhyate | śānti-khaḍgaḥ kare yasya kiṁ kariṣyati durjanaḥ ||
translation: Forgiveness is the means of subduing the world; what cannot be achieved by forgiveness? He who holds the sword of peace — what can the wicked do to him?
meaning: The power of forgiveness and inner peace is mightier than any weapon — the truly calm person cannot be harmed by the malicious.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अयं निजः परो वेति गणना लघुचेतसाम् । उदारचरितानां तु वसुधैव कुटुम्बकम् ॥
transliteration: ayaṁ nijaḥ paro veti gaṇanā laghu-cetasām | udāra-caritānāṁ tu vasudhaiva kuṭumbakam ||
translation: This is mine, this is another's — such calculation is for the narrow-minded. For the broad-hearted, the entire earth is one family.
meaning: The famous phrase Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam — the world is one family — reflects the highest vision of human unity.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अग्निः शेषं कृतं येन ऋणशेषं कृतं तथा । रोगशेषं कृतं येन तस्य शेषं विशेषतः ॥
transliteration: agniḥ śeṣaṁ kṛtaṁ yena ṛṇa-śeṣaṁ kṛtaṁ tathā | roga-śeṣaṁ kṛtaṁ yena tasya śeṣaṁ viśeṣataḥ ||
translation: One who leaves fire, debt, or disease only partially handled — from that partial remainder, destruction will come.
meaning: Never leave fire, debt, or illness partially dealt with — they grow and destroy if left incomplete.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एकं हन्यान्न वा हन्यादिषुर्मुक्तो धनुष्मता । बुद्धिर्बुद्धिमतोत्सृष्टा हन्ति राष्ट्रं सराजकम् ॥
transliteration: ekaṁ hanyān na vā hanyād iṣur mukto dhanuṣmatā | buddhir buddhi-matosṛṣṭā hanti rāṣṭraṁ sa-rājakam ||
translation: An arrow shot may kill one or none; but intellect wielded by an intelligent person can destroy an entire kingdom including its king.
meaning: The weapon of intelligence and strategy is far more powerful than any physical weapon.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: विद्या विवादाय धनं मदाय शक्तिः परेषां परिपीडनाय । खलस्य साधोर्विपरीतमेतज्ज्ञानाय दानाय च रक्षणाय ॥
transliteration: vidyā vivādāya dhanaṁ madāya śaktiḥ pareṣāṁ paripīḍanāya | khālasya sādhor viparītam etaj jñānāya dānāya ca rakṣaṇāya ||
translation: For a wicked person, education leads to argument, wealth to arrogance, and power to oppression. For the virtuous it is the reverse — education for knowledge, wealth for giving, power for protection.
meaning: The same resources produce opposite effects based on character — the virtuous use them for good, the wicked for harm.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: गुरुरग्निर्द्विजातीनां वर्णानां ब्राह्मणो गुरुः । पतिरेव गुरुः स्त्रीणां सर्वस्याभ्यागतो गुरुः ॥
transliteration: gurur agnir dvijātīnāṁ varṇānāṁ brāhmaṇo guruḥ | patir eva guruḥ strīṇāṁ sarvasyābhyāgato guruḥ ||
translation: Fire is the guru of the twice-born; the Brahmin is the guru of all classes; the husband is the guru of women; and a guest is the guru of all.
meaning: All relationships can be teacher-student relationships — even a guest arriving at one's home brings lessons.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यथा धेनुसहस्रेषु वत्सो गच्छति मातरम् । तथा पूर्वकृतं कर्म कर्तारमनुगच्छति ॥
transliteration: yathā dhenu-sahasreṣu vatso gacchati mātaram | tathā pūrva-kṛtaṁ karma kartāram anugacchati ||
translation: As a calf among a thousand cows finds its own mother, so the karma performed finds its doer.
meaning: No karma goes unaccounted — every action finds its way back to the one who performed it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अर्थार्थी जीवलोकोऽयं शाश्वतं नास्ति किञ्चन । प्राणिनः स्वार्थसंयुक्ताः स्वार्थं देहमपेक्षते ॥
transliteration: arthārthī jīva-loko'yaṁ śāśvataṁ nāsti kiñcana | prāṇinaḥ svārtha-saṁyuktāḥ svārthaṁ deham apekṣate ||
translation: The living world seeks material gain; nothing is permanent. All beings are united by self-interest, and they look to the body for their interests.
meaning: Pragmatic realism: most worldly action is driven by self-interest; recognizing this is key to navigating human nature.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: राज्ञः प्रियहिते युक्तो मन्त्री राजन्यतां व्रजेत् । देवः प्रसन्नः सन्तुष्टः कार्याण्यासाद्य साधयेत् ॥
transliteration: rājñaḥ priya-hite yukto mantrī rājanyatāṁ vrajet | devaḥ prasannaḥ santuṣṭaḥ kāryāṇy āsādya sādhayet ||
translation: A minister devoted to the king's interests and welfare attains royalty. The pleased and contented deity accomplishes works by making them accessible.
meaning: Loyal and wise counsel elevates a minister; and grace comes to those who are satisfied and devoted.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यस्मिन्देशे न सम्मानो न वृत्तिर्न च बान्धवाः । न च विद्यागमः कश्चित्तं देशं परिवर्जयेत् ॥
transliteration: yasmin deśe na sammāno na vṛttir na ca bāndhavāḥ | na ca vidyāgamaḥ kaścit taṁ deśaṁ parvarjayet ||
translation: One should leave a country where there is no honor, no livelihood, no relatives, and no possibility of gaining knowledge.
meaning: Practical advice: do not stay in a place that offers neither respect, sustenance, community, nor opportunity for growth.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: तद् भोजनं यद् द्विजभुक्तशेषं तत्सौहृदं यत्क्रियते परार्थम् । सा प्रज्ञा या न प्रविशेत्कुतर्के सा दीक्षा या दुःखविमोक्षणाय ॥
transliteration: tad bhojanaṁ yad dvija-bhukta-śeṣaṁ tat saukhyaṁ yat kriyate parārtham | sā prajñā yā na praviśet kutarke sā dīkṣā yā duḥkha-vimokṣaṇāya ||
translation: True food is the remnants of a Brahmin's meal; true friendship is acting for another's sake; true wisdom is that which does not enter into false arguments; true initiation is that which frees one from suffering.
meaning: Chanakya defines what is truly nourishing, friendly, wise, and liberating in deeper terms.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: परोक्षे कार्यहन्तारं प्रत्यक्षे प्रियवादिनम् । वर्जयेत्तादृशं मित्रं विषकुम्भं पयोमुखम् ॥
transliteration: parokṣe kārya-hantāraṁ pratyakṣe priya-vādinam | varjayet tādṛśaṁ mitraṁ viṣa-kumbhaṁ payo-mukham ||
translation: Avoid a friend who speaks sweetly to your face but destroys your work behind your back — like a pot of poison with milk on top.
meaning: A friend who praises you openly but undermines you secretly is your worst enemy — their sweetness hides poison.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: न कश्चिद्यस्य नो मित्रं नो शत्रुः सर्वथा नरः । अर्थी मित्रं करोत्याशु ह्यनर्थी शत्रुतां व्रजेत् ॥
transliteration: na kaścid yasya no mitraṁ no śatruḥ sarvathā naraḥ | arthī mitraṁ karoty āśu hy anarthī śatrutāṁ vrajet ||
translation: No person has a permanent friend or permanent enemy. The interested person becomes a friend quickly; the one who serves no purpose becomes an enemy.
meaning: In worldly affairs, friendship and enmity are rarely permanent — self-interest shapes both.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यं मातापितरौ क्लेशं सहेते सम्भवे नृणाम् । न तस्य निष्कृतिः शक्या कर्तुं वर्षशतैरपि ॥
transliteration: yaṁ mātāpitarau kleśaṁ sahete sambhave nṛṇām | na tasya niṣkṛtiḥ śakyā kartuṁ varṣa-śatair api ||
translation: The pain that parents undergo in giving birth to children cannot be repaid even in a hundred years.
meaning: The sacrifice of parents is beyond repayment — gratitude toward them is one of life's paramount duties.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: लुब्धमर्थेन गृह्णीयात्स्तब्धमञ्जलिकर्मणा । मूर्खं छन्दानुरोधेन यथार्थत्वेन पण्डितम् ॥
transliteration: lubdham arthena gṛhṇīyāt stabdham añjali-karmaṇā | mūrkhaṁ chandānurodhena yathārthatvena paṇḍitam ||
translation: Win over the greedy with money; the arrogant with folded hands; the fool by agreeing with him; and the wise with truth.
meaning: Different people require different approaches — strategy must match the nature of the person you are dealing with.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: हस्ती अङ्कुशमात्रेण वाजी हस्तेन तारणः । शृङ्गी लगुडहस्तेन खड्गहस्तेन दुर्जनः ॥
transliteration: hastī aṅkuśa-mātreṇa vājī hastena tāraṇaḥ | śṛṅgī laguḍa-hastena khaḍga-hastena durjanaḥ ||
translation: An elephant is controlled with an ankusha (goad); a horse by hand; a horned animal by a stick; and a wicked person by a sword.
meaning: Different creatures and people require different means of control — there is no one-size-fits-all approach to authority.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: विप्रयोर्विप्रविप्रयोर्वह्निब्राह्मणयोस्तथा । दम्पत्योः स्वामिभृत्ययोर्मध्ये न हि विचारयेत् ॥
transliteration: viprayor vipra-viprayor vahni-brāhmaṇayos tathā | dampatyoḥ svāmi-bhṛtyayor madhye na hi vicārayet ||
translation: Never interfere between two Brahmins, a Brahmin and fire, husband and wife, or master and servant.
meaning: Avoid meddling in other people's relationships and conflicts — it only brings trouble to the one who interferes.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: दानेन पाणिर्न तु कङ्कणेन स्नानेन शुद्धिर्न तु चन्दनेन । मानेन तृप्तिर्न तु भोजनेन ज्ञानेन मुक्तिर्न तु मण्डनेन ॥
transliteration: dānena pāṇir na tu kaṅkaṇena snānena śuddhir na tu candanena | mānena tṛptir na tu bhojanena jñānena muktir na tu maṇḍanena ||
translation: The hand is graced by charity, not by a bracelet; purity comes from bathing, not from sandalwood paste; satisfaction comes from self-respect, not food; liberation comes from knowledge, not adornment.
meaning: Inner realities — charity, purity, dignity, liberation — cannot be substituted by outer appearances.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: पृथिव्यां त्रीणि रत्नानि जलमन्नं सुभाषितम् । मूढैः पाषाणखण्डेषु रत्नसंज्ञा प्रदीयते ॥
transliteration: pṛthivyāṁ trīṇi ratnāni jalam annaṁ subhāṣitam | mūḍhaiḥ pāṣāṇa-khaṇḍeṣu ratna-saṁjñā pradīyate ||
translation: There are three gems on this earth: water, food, and wise words. Fools call pieces of stone gems.
meaning: What truly sustains life is water, food, and wisdom — people who chase material gems miss what is genuinely valuable.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आरोग्यं बुद्धिमान् राजा नारी च प्रियवादिनी । वत्सलं च वने वृक्षं दुर्लभं हि चतुष्टयम् ॥
transliteration: ārogyaṁ buddhimān rājā nārī ca priya-vādinī | vatsalaṁ ca vane vṛkṣaṁ durlabhaṁ hi catuṣṭayam ||
translation: Four things are rare: good health, a wise king, a pleasant-speaking wife, and a fruit-bearing tree in the forest.
meaning: Chanakya identifies four rare blessings that are genuinely difficult to find in the world.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: जनिता चोपनेता च यस्तु विद्यां प्रयच्छति । अन्नदाता भयत्राता पञ्चैते पितरः स्मृताः ॥
transliteration: janitā copaneta ca yas tu vidyāṁ prayacchati | anna-dātā bhaya-trātā pañcaite pitaraḥ smṛtāḥ ||
translation: Five are remembered as fathers: the biological father, the one who performs the thread ceremony, the one who gives knowledge, the one who gives food, and the one who protects from fear.
meaning: Fatherhood is not only biological — those who give us education, sustenance, initiation, and protection are all fathers.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: राजा राष्ट्रकृतं पापं राज्ञः पापं पुरोहितः । भर्ता च स्त्रीकृतं पापं शिष्यपापं गुरुस्तथा ॥
transliteration: rājā rāṣṭra-kṛtaṁ pāpaṁ rājñaḥ pāpaṁ purohitaḥ | bhartā ca strī-kṛtaṁ pāpaṁ śiṣya-pāpaṁ gurus tathā ||
translation: The king bears the sin of the nation, the priest bears the sin of the king, the husband bears the sin of the wife, and the teacher bears the sin of the student.
meaning: Leadership implies responsibility — those in charge share in the moral consequences of those under their care.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: वरयेत्कुलजां प्राज्ञो विरूपामपि कन्यकाम् । रूपशीलां न नीचस्य विवाहः सदृशे कुले ॥
transliteration: vārayet kulajāṁ prājño virūpām api kanyakām | rūpa-śīlāṁ na nīcasya vivāhaḥ sadṛśe kule ||
translation: The wise man should choose a girl of good family even if she is plain-looking, not a beautiful girl of low family. Marriage should be among equals.
meaning: Character, family background, and values are more important in marriage than physical appearance.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: नद्यः पूर्णाः नदन्ति नैव मेघाः पूर्णाः तथा न गर्जन्ति । विद्वान्पूर्णो न मन्येत साधवः स्वयमेव संजानन्ति ॥
transliteration: nadyaḥ pūrṇāḥ nadanti naiva meghāḥ pūrṇāḥ tathā na garjanti | vidvān-pūrṇo na manyeta sādhavaḥ svayam eva saṁjānanti ||
translation: Full rivers flow quietly; full clouds do not thunder loudly. A truly full (learned) person does not boast; the virtuous know their own worth.
meaning: True abundance and wisdom are silent and unassuming — it is emptiness that makes the most noise.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एते सद्गुणाः सर्वे येषां धृतिः समाहिता । सत्यं च मृदुवाक्यं च ह्रीः शौचं अनसूयता ॥
transliteration: ete sad-guṇāḥ sarve yeṣāṁ dhṛtiḥ samāhitā | satyaṁ ca mṛdu-vākyaṁ ca hrīḥ śaucaṁ anasūyatā ||
translation: These are all good qualities: steadiness, truth, gentle speech, modesty, purity, and freedom from envy.
meaning: The six qualities that define a person of good character — these are the foundation of a virtuous life.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अर्थस्य पुरुषो दासो दासस्त्वर्थो न कस्यचित् । इति मत्वा महाराज सुखं अर्थं च साधय ॥
transliteration: arthasya puruṣo dāso dāsas tv artho na kasyacit | iti matvā mahārāja sukhaṁ arthaṁ ca sādhaya ||
translation: Man is a slave of wealth, but wealth is no one's slave. Knowing this, O king, pursue both happiness and wealth.
meaning: Wealth is a means, not a master — the wise person masters wealth rather than being mastered by it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आत्मापराधवृक्षस्य फलान्येतानि देहिनाम् । दारिद्र्यरोगदुःखानि बन्धनव्यसनानि च ॥
transliteration: ātmāparādha-vṛkṣasya phalāny etāni dehinām | dāridrya-roga-duḥkhāni bandhana-vyasanāni ca ||
translation: Poverty, disease, sorrow, imprisonment, and addiction — these are the fruits of the tree of one's own sins.
meaning: Most misfortune is the fruit of our own past mistakes — taking responsibility is the first step to change.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: नमस्कुर्वन्ति देवेभ्यो धर्मे च रतमानसाः । प्रसीदन्ति महात्मानो यत्र तिष्ठत्यहिंसता ॥
transliteration: namaskurvanti devebhyo dharme ca ratamānasāḥ | prasīdanti mahātmāno yatra tiṣṭhaty ahiṁsatā ||
translation: They who bow to the gods and whose minds are absorbed in dharma — great souls rejoice wherever non-violence prevails.
meaning: Devotion, dharma, and non-violence are the three pillars that draw the blessing of great souls.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: येन केनाप्युपायेन मित्रत्वं कारयेत्सदा । भेदाभेदौ विनश्येते यत्र मैत्री स्थिरा भवेत् ॥
transliteration: yena kenāpy upāyena mitratvaṁ kārayet sadā | bhedābhedau vinaśyete yatra maitrī sthirā bhavet ||
translation: Friendship should be cultivated by any and all means. Where stable friendship exists, division and conflict perish.
meaning: Friendship is the most powerful antidote to conflict — invest in it by all possible means.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: मृगाः मृगैः संगमनुव्रजन्ति गावश्च गोभिस्तुरगास्तुरङ्गैः । मूर्खाश्च मूर्खैः सुधियः सुधीभिः समानशीला व्यसनेषु सक्ताः ॥
transliteration: mṛgāḥ mṛgaiḥ saṁgam anuvrajanti gāvaś ca gobhis turagās turaṅgaiḥ | mūrkhāś ca mūrkhaiḥ sudhiyaḥ sudhībhiḥ samāna-śīlā vyasaneṣu saktāḥ ||
translation: Deer seek deer, cows seek cows, horses seek horses; fools seek fools, and the wise seek the wise. Like always finds like.
meaning: We naturally gravitate to those who are like us — choose your company wisely, for it shapes your character.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अति रूपेण वै सीता चाति गर्वेण रावणः । अति दानाद् बलिर्बद्धो ह्यति सर्वत्र वर्जयेत् ॥
transliteration: ati rūpeṇa vai sītā cāti garveṇa rāvaṇaḥ | ati dānād balir baddho hy ati sarvatra varjayet ||
translation: Sita was abducted because of her beauty; Ravana fell because of his arrogance; Bali was bound because of excessive giving. Excess in all things should be avoided.
meaning: Even virtues carried to excess can cause downfall. Balance is wisdom.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: को हि सर्पः समो जन्तुर्यः क्रुद्धः सर्पवद्दशेत् । मन्त्रौषधिवशः सर्पः दुर्जनस्तु न वश्यते ॥
transliteration: ko hi sarpaḥ samo jantur yaḥ kruddhaḥ sarpavad daśet | mantrauṣadhi-vaśaḥ sarpaḥ durjanas tu na vaśyate ||
translation: Who is more dangerous than a snake? The angry person who bites like a snake. A snake can be controlled by mantras and medicine; a wicked person cannot be controlled.
meaning: Human malice is harder to manage than even a venomous snake — at least the snake responds to charms.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: वृक्षाग्रेवासिनः पक्षी न भयेन न विह्वलाः । सन्त्येवंभाविनः सन्तः सदा स्वस्थाश्चिरायुषः ॥
transliteration: vṛkṣāgre vāsinaḥ pakṣī na bhayena na vihvalāḥ | santy evaṁ bhāvinaḥ santaḥ sadā svasthāś cirāyuṣaḥ ||
translation: Birds perched at the tops of trees are not fearful or distressed — similarly the virtuous who are unattached are ever healthy and long-lived.
meaning: Equanimity and non-attachment — like a bird perched lightly — lead to health and longevity.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: किमत्र चित्रं यदि राजपुत्रो जयेद्रणे शस्त्रसमाकुले । क्षमासमुद्रो भवतीति शूरः स्वशत्रुषु प्रीतिरसो विधत्ते ॥
transliteration: kim atra citraṁ yadi rāja-putro jayed raṇe śastra-samākule | kṣamā-samudro bhavatīti śūraḥ sva-śatruṣu prīti-raso vidhatte ||
translation: What is remarkable if a prince wins a battle? The true hero is one who is an ocean of forgiveness and shows affection even toward enemies.
meaning: Military victory is ordinary; the extraordinary feat is to forgive and show compassion even to enemies.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: जलबिन्दुनिपातेन क्रमशः पूर्यते घटः । स हेतुः सर्वविद्यानां धर्मस्य च धनस्य च ॥
transliteration: jala-bindu-nipātena kramaśaḥ pūryate ghaṭaḥ | sa hetuḥ sarva-vidyānāṁ dharmasya ca dhanasya ca ||
translation: A pot is filled drop by drop; this is the reason for acquiring all knowledge, dharma, and wealth.
meaning: All great accumulations — knowledge, virtue, wealth — happen gradually, one small effort at a time.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अनालोच्य व्ययं कृत्वा ह्यनागत्य विचिन्तयेत् । विनाशकालं आगत्य लक्ष्मीः विलयमायाति ॥
transliteration: anālocya vyayaṁ kṛtvā hy anāgatya vicintayet | vināśa-kālaṁ āgatya lakṣmīḥ vilayam āyāti ||
translation: Those who spend without thinking and do not plan for the future — when the time of ruin arrives, their wealth vanishes.
meaning: Financial ruin comes from thoughtless spending and lack of foresight. Plan ahead and spend wisely.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: उत्साहसम्पन्नमदीर्घसूत्रं क्रियाविधज्ञं व्यसनेष्वसक्तम् । शूरं कृतज्ञं दृढसौहृदं च लक्ष्मीः स्वयं याति निवासहेतोः ॥
transliteration: utsāha-sampannam adīrgha-sūtraṁ kriyā-vidha-jñaṁ vyasaneṣv asaktam | śūraṁ kṛtajñaṁ dṛḍha-sauhṛdaṁ ca lakṣmīḥ svayam yāti nivāsa-hetoḥ ||
translation: Wealth itself comes to dwell with one who is enthusiastic, not a procrastinator, wise in action, free from addiction, courageous, grateful, and firm in friendship.
meaning: These seven qualities — enthusiasm, promptness, practical wisdom, freedom from vice, courage, gratitude, and loyalty — naturally attract prosperity.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: बलं विद्या च विप्राणां राज्ञां सैन्यं बलं तथा । बलं वित्तं च वैश्यानां शूद्राणां परिचर्यकम् ॥
transliteration: balaṁ vidyā ca viprāṇāṁ rājñāṁ sainyaṁ balaṁ tathā | balaṁ vittaṁ ca vaiśyānāṁ śūdrāṇāṁ paricaryakam ||
translation: The strength of a Brahmin is knowledge; the strength of a king is his army; the strength of a merchant is his wealth; the strength of a Shudra is service.
meaning: Each class derives its power from its core function — know your own strength and cultivate it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: निर्धनः पुरुषो नीचो मूर्खश्चापि न पूजितः । निष्कुले जायते पापं धनिकः सर्वत्र पूजितः ॥
transliteration: nirdhanaḥ puruṣo nīco mūrkhaś cāpi na pūjitaḥ | niṣkule jāyate pāpaṁ dhanikaḥ sarvatra pūjitaḥ ||
translation: A poor man is considered low-born, a fool is not respected, and sin arises from low family — but a rich man is respected everywhere.
meaning: Chanakya realistically observes society's bias toward wealth, without necessarily endorsing it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सुचिरं मधुरं वाक्यमुत्तमं यत्कृतं परैः । उपकारश्च संस्मार्यः संस्मार्यं च व्यतिक्रमेत् ॥
transliteration: suciraṁ madhuraṁ vākyam uttamaṁ yat kṛtaṁ paraiḥ | upakāraś ca saṁsmāryaḥ saṁsmāryaṁ ca vyatikramet ||
translation: Sweet words said long ago, acts of kindness done by others — these should be remembered. But wrongs received should be forgotten.
meaning: Remember kindnesses; forget wrongs. This is the practice of a noble heart.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: न दुर्जनः साधुदशामुपैति बहुप्रकारैरपि शिक्षयन्नम् । आमूलसिक्तः पयसा घृतेन न निम्बवृक्षो मधुरत्वमेति ॥
transliteration: na durjanaḥ sādhu-daśām upaiti bahu-prakārair api śikṣayann am | āmūla-siktaḥ payasā ghṛtena na nimba-vṛkṣo madhuratvam eti ||
translation: A wicked person does not become virtuous despite being taught in many ways — a neem tree watered with milk and ghee from the roots does not become sweet.
meaning: Fundamental character cannot be changed by external improvements — a neem tree remains bitter no matter how you nurture it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: उत्तमा सौम्यसंयुक्ता मध्यमा क्रियासंयुता । कनिष्ठा कलहप्रिया नारीणां त्रिविधा गतिः ॥
transliteration: uttamā saumya-saṁyuktā madhyamā kriyā-saṁyutā | kaniṣṭhā kalaha-priyā nārīṇāṁ trividhā gatiḥ ||
translation: There are three kinds of women: the best are gentle and composed; the middle are active and industrious; the worst are quarrelsome.
meaning: Women (and by extension, people) are classified by their dominant disposition: equanimity, activity, or conflict-seeking.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: प्रत्यहं प्रत्यवेक्षेत नरश्चरितमात्मनः । किं मया पापमद्याभूत्किं वा साधु कृतं मया ॥
transliteration: pratyahaṁ pratyavekṣeta naraś caritam ātmanaḥ | kiṁ mayā pāpam adyābhūt kiṁ vā sādhu kṛtaṁ mayā ||
translation: Every day one should review one's own conduct: what wrong did I do today? What good did I do today?
meaning: Daily self-reflection on one's actions is essential to moral growth and self-correction.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: तद्याति वन्द्यतां लोके यच्चरित्रं न दुष्यति । क्षणे क्षणे यन्नवता उपैति तदेव रूपं रमणीयतायाः ॥
transliteration: tad yāti vandyatāṁ loke yac caritraṁ na duṣyati | kṣaṇe kṣaṇe yan navatā upaiti tad eva rūpaṁ ramaṇīyatāyāḥ ||
translation: That person becomes worthy of worship in the world whose character is untarnished. That beauty is true beauty which grows fresher moment by moment.
meaning: True beauty and worthiness come from spotless character that renews itself continuously.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यो मोहान्मन्यते मूढो रक्षितव्यमिति स्वकम् । स हि तस्यैव वशगो भवत्यात्मपरिग्रहः ॥
transliteration: yo mohān manyate mūḍho rakṣitavyam iti svakam | sa hi tasyaiva vaśago bhavaty ātma-parigrahaḥ ||
translation: The fool who thinks 'I must protect what is mine' — he himself becomes a slave to that very possessiveness.
meaning: Attachment to possessions makes one their slave — the very act of protecting creates bondage.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अनारम्भो हि कार्याणां प्रथमं बुद्धिलक्षणम् । प्रारब्धस्यान्तगमनं द्वितीयं बुद्धिलक्षणम् ॥
transliteration: anārambho hi kāryāṇāṁ prathamaṁ buddhi-lakṣaṇam | prārabdhasyānta-gamanaṁ dvitīyaṁ buddhi-lakṣaṇam ||
translation: The first sign of wisdom is not beginning an unworthy task; the second sign is completing what has been begun.
meaning: Wisdom is shown first by discernment — not starting what shouldn't be started — and second by perseverance — finishing what was rightly begun.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: उपार्जितानां वित्तानां त्याग एव हि रक्षणम् । तडागोदरसंस्थानां परीवाह इवाम्भसाम् ॥
transliteration: upārjitānāṁ vittānāṁ tyāga eva hi rakṣaṇam | taḍāgodara-saṁsthānāṁ parīvāha ivāmbhasām ||
translation: The protection of accumulated wealth is through giving it away — like water in a tank is protected by an overflow outlet.
meaning: Wealth that flows through giving remains pure and growing; hoarded wealth stagnates like still water.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: तस्मान्न केनचिद्भावो नाभावो वापि रोचते । केचिन्मध्यं विचिन्त्यन्ति ज्ञानिनः सर्वदर्शिनः ॥
transliteration: tasmān na kenacid bhāvo nābhāvo vāpi rocate | kecin madhyaṁ vicintyanti jñāninaḥ sarvdarśinaḥ ||
translation: Therefore, the all-seeing wise find neither existence nor non-existence pleasing — they reflect on the middle path.
meaning: The truly wise transcend both attachment to existence and aversion to non-existence — they walk the middle path.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यस्य चेतसि सत्त्वं स्यात् यस्य शीलं स्यात् गुणान्वितम् । तस्यैव भूषणं रूपं रूपं हीनस्य भूषणम् ॥
transliteration: yasya cetasi sattvaṁ syāt yasya śīlaṁ syāt guṇānvitam | tasyaiva bhūṣaṇaṁ rūpaṁ rūpaṁ hīnasya bhūṣaṇam ||
translation: For one who has a pure mind and a character filled with virtues, physical beauty becomes an ornament. For the one without these, beauty is the only ornament.
meaning: Inner virtue enhances outer beauty; for those lacking virtue, physical beauty is their only adornment.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: विद्या मित्रं प्रवासे च भार्या मित्रं गृहेषु च । व्याधितस्यौषधं मित्रं धर्मो मित्रं मृतस्य च ॥
transliteration: vidyā mitraṁ pravāse ca bhāryā mitraṁ gṛheṣu ca | vyādhitasyauṣadhaṁ mitraṁ dharmo mitraṁ mṛtasya ca ||
translation: Knowledge is a friend when traveling; a wife is a friend at home; medicine is a friend in illness; and dharma is a friend after death.
meaning: Different situations call for different supports — knowledge, family, health, and virtue each serve as our companions at different times.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: न काश्चित् कस्यचित् मित्रं न काश्चित् कस्यचित् रिपुः । अर्थतस्तु निबध्यन्ते मित्राणि रिपवस्तथा ॥
transliteration: na kāścit kasyacit mitraṁ na kāścit kasyacid ripuḥ | arthathas tu nibadhyante mitrāṇi ripavaś ca ||
translation: No one is anyone's inherent friend; no one is anyone's inherent enemy. Friends and enemies are created by circumstances of interest.
meaning: In the pragmatic world of politics, friendship and enmity are defined by interest, not by nature.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: शुभं करोति कल्याणमारोग्यं धनसम्पदः। शत्रुबुद्धिविनाशाय दीपज्योतिर्नमोऽस्तुते ॥
transliteration: śubhaṁ karoti kalyāṇam ārogyaṁ dhana-sampadaḥ | śatru-buddhi-vināśāya dīpa-jyotir namo'stu te ||
translation: May the lamp's light bring auspiciousness, well-being, good health, wealth, and destruction of the enemy's intellect — I bow to the light of the lamp.
meaning: This prayer to the lamp light invokes blessings of all kinds — it is still chanted in Indian homes today.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एतद्विद्यासमुद्दिष्टं न वाच्यं यस्य कस्यचित् । सुरक्षितं महाराज ग्राहयेत्सुशिष्यतः ॥
transliteration: etad vidyā-samuddiṣṭaṁ na vācyaṁ yasya kasyacit | surakṣitaṁ mahārāja grāhayet suśiṣyataḥ ||
translation: This knowledge should not be revealed to just anyone; it should be protected and transmitted only to a worthy student.
meaning: Sacred or strategic knowledge must be guarded and given only to those who are truly prepared for it.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अकारणाच्च यः क्रोधाद् धिक्करोति गुरुं जनः। क्षिप्रं स विनशेत् सर्वं ब्रह्महत्यासमं हि तत् ॥
transliteration: akāraṇāc ca yaḥ krodhād dhik karoti guruṁ janaḥ | kṣipraṁ sa vinaśet sarvaṁ brahma-hatyā-samaṁ hi tat ||
translation: One who disrespects a teacher out of causeless anger perishes quickly — it is equivalent to the sin of killing a Brahmin.
meaning: Disrespect for one's teacher is considered the gravest transgression, bringing swift ruin.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: व्यये कृते वर्धते एव नित्यं विद्याधनं सर्वधनप्रधानम् । क्षयमायाति न कदाचिदेव तस्माद्विद्याधनं श्रेष्ठमेव ॥
transliteration: vyaye kṛte vardhate eva nityaṁ vidyā-dhanaṁ sarva-dhana-pradhānam | kṣayam āyāti na kadācid eva tasmād vidyā-dhanaṁ śreṣṭham eva ||
translation: The wealth of knowledge grows when spent — it is the foremost of all wealth. It never diminishes. Therefore, the wealth of knowledge is truly the best.
meaning: Knowledge is the only wealth that grows when shared — it cannot be stolen, burned, or divided. It is the highest wealth.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: दयालुत्वं क्षमावृत्तिः सत्यं शौचं विशेषतः । इन्द्रियजयो विद्या च अहिंसा च द्विजातिषु ॥
transliteration: dayālutvaṁ kṣamā-vṛttiḥ satyaṁ śaucaṁ viśeṣataḥ | indriya-jayo vidyā ca ahiṁsā ca dvijātiṣu ||
translation: Compassion, forgiveness, truth, purity especially, control of the senses, learning, and non-violence — these are special qualities of the twice-born.
meaning: These seven virtues define the ideal conduct of an educated, spiritually initiated person.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: दानं भोगो नाशस्तिस्रो गतयो भवन्ति वित्तस्य । यो न ददाति न भुङ्क्ते तस्य तृतीया गतिर्भवति ॥
transliteration: dānaṁ bhogo nāśas tisro gatayo bhavanti vittasya | yo na dadāti na bhuṅkte tasya tṛtīyā gatir bhavati ||
translation: Wealth has three paths: charity, enjoyment, and destruction. One who neither gives nor enjoys — for him the third path is certain.
meaning: Wealth must be either given or enjoyed — hoarded wealth ultimately just destroys itself and its owner.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आरम्भगुर्वी क्षयिणी क्रमेण लघ्वी पुरा वृद्धिमती च पश्चात् । दिनस्य पूर्वार्धपरार्धभिन्ना छायेव मैत्री खलसज्जनानाम् ॥
transliteration: ārambha-gurvī kṣayiṇī krameṇa laghvī purā vṛddhimatī ca paścāt | dinasya pūrvārdha-parārdha-bhinnā chāyeva maitrī khala-sajjanānām ||
translation: The friendship of the wicked is great at the start, then diminishes; the friendship of the good is small at first, then grows — like the shadow of a tree in the morning and evening.
meaning: Bad friendships shine brightly but fade; good friendships start quietly and deepen over time, like the shadow of a tree.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अनेकसंशयोच्छेदी परोक्षार्थस्य दर्शकम् । सर्वस्य लोचनं शास्त्रं यस्य नास्त्यन्ध एव सः ॥
transliteration: aneka-saṁśayoccheddī parokṣārthasya darśakam | sarvasya locanaṁ śāstraṁ yasya nāsty andha eva saḥ ||
translation: Scripture removes many doubts, reveals the unseen, and is the eye of all. One without it is truly blind.
meaning: Sacred knowledge is our eye for the invisible — without it, we are blind to the deeper dimensions of reality.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: परहस्ते गते चित्ते न विद्या न च पौरुषम् । तस्मात् स्वस्थचित्तस्य सर्वं सम्पत्स्यते शुभम् ॥
transliteration: para-haste gate citte na vidyā na ca pauruṣam | tasmāt svastha-cittasya sarvaṁ sampatsyate śubham ||
translation: When the mind is in another's control, there is neither knowledge nor manliness. Therefore, everything auspicious comes to one whose mind is at rest.
meaning: Mental independence is the foundation of both wisdom and courage. A dependent or disturbed mind cannot achieve either.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: जन्म दारिद्र्यभीतिश्च मृत्युश्च नरकं तथा । दुर्जनस्य गृहे वासः पञ्चाग्नयो न संशयः ॥
transliteration: janma dāridrya-bhītiś ca mṛtyuś ca narakaṁ tathā | durjanasya gṛhe vāsaḥ pañcāgnayo na saṁśayaḥ ||
translation: Birth, fear of poverty, death, hell, and living in a wicked person's house — these five are like hellfire, without doubt.
meaning: The miseries of life are many, but living at the mercy of a wicked person is among the most painful.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सर्वेषामेव शौचानां अर्थशौचं परं स्मृतम् । यः शुचिः स शुचिः प्रोक्तो न मृद्वारिशुचिः शुचिः ॥
transliteration: sarveṣām eva śaucānāṁ artha-śaucaṁ paraṁ smṛtam | yaḥ śuciḥ sa śuciḥ prokto na mṛd-vāri-śuciḥ śuciḥ ||
translation: Among all forms of purity, purity in one's means of livelihood is the highest. The truly pure person is called pure by the wise — not one who is merely clean with water and earth.
meaning: Inner purity and honest livelihood surpass any external ritual cleanliness.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: धनहीनो न हीनः स्यात् धनिको नैव पूजितः । तेजस्वी शूरवीरश्च स पूज्यः सर्वदेहिभिः ॥
transliteration: dhana-hīno na hīnaḥ syāt dhaniko naiva pūjitaḥ | tejasvī śūra-vīraś ca sa pūjyaḥ sarva-dehibhiḥ ||
translation: The poor man is not really poor; the rich man is not truly worthy of worship. The person of brilliance and valor is truly worthy of reverence by all beings.
meaning: True worth is not measured by wealth but by inner radiance, courage, and virtue.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: पूजनीया महाभागाः पुण्याश्च गृहदीप्तयः । स्त्रियो रत्नान्यभरणान्यसाधारणमेव हि ॥
transliteration: pūjanīyā mahābhāgāḥ puṇyāś ca gṛha-dīptayaḥ | striyo ratnāny abharaṇāny asādhāraṇam eva hi ||
translation: Extraordinary women are worthy of worship — they are blessed, light up the household, and are jewels and ornaments unlike any other.
meaning: Virtuous women who are devoted and enlightened are the true ornaments of a household and society.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: गृहे शत्रुं न वासयेत् पुत्रं शिक्षयेत् सदा । गृहकर्म न कुर्वन्तं भृत्यं भर्त्सयेत् सदा ॥
transliteration: gṛhe śatruṁ na vāsayet putraṁ śikṣayet sadā | gṛha-karma na kurvantaṁ bhṛtyaṁ bhartsayet sadā ||
translation: Do not harbor an enemy in your house; always educate your son; always reprimand a servant who does not do his work.
meaning: Household management requires firmness: no shelter for enemies, constant guidance for children, and accountability for servants.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एको वशी सर्वभूतान्तरात्मा एकं बीजं बहुधा यः करोति । तमात्मस्थं येऽनुपश्यन्ति धीरास्तेषां शान्तिः शाश्वती नेतरेषाम् ॥
transliteration: eko vaśī sarva-bhūtāntarātmā ekaṁ bījaṁ bahudhā yaḥ karoti | tam ātmasthaṁ ye'nupaśyanti dhīrās teṣāṁ śāntiḥ śāśvatī netareṣām ||
translation: The one Self, dwelling in all beings, the single seed that manifests in many forms — those steadfast ones who see It within themselves attain eternal peace; others do not.
meaning: This verse (also found in Katha Upanishad) indicates that Chanakya drew from Vedantic wisdom. Inner vision of the Self is the path to lasting peace.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सत्यात् नास्ति परो धर्मः मिथ्यात् नास्ति पातकम् । वित्त नास्ति परं क्षेमं दानात् नास्ति सुखं परम् ॥
transliteration: satyāt nāsti paro dharmaḥ mithyāt nāsti pātakam | vitta nāsti paraṁ kṣemaṁ dānāt nāsti sukhaṁ param ||
translation: There is no dharma higher than truth; there is no sin greater than falsehood; there is no welfare greater than virtue; and there is no happiness greater than giving.
meaning: Truth, virtue, and generosity are the highest human values according to Chanakya.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अनर्थं अर्थमित्येव मन्यते मूढचेतनः। अर्थमनर्थमित्येव मन्यते पण्डितस्तथा ॥
transliteration: anarthaṁ artham ity eva manyate mūḍha-cetanaḥ | artham anartham ity eva manyate paṇḍitas tathā ||
translation: The fool considers what is harmful to be beneficial; the wise man considers what appears beneficial to be potentially harmful.
meaning: Wisdom means looking beyond appearances — what seems profitable may be harmful, and vice versa.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: उद्यमेन हि सिद्ध्यन्ति कार्याणि न मनोरथैः । न हि सुप्तस्य सिंहस्य प्रविशन्ति मुखे मृगाः ॥
transliteration: udyamena hi sidhyanti kāryāṇi na manorathaiḥ | na hi suptasya siṁhasya praviśanti mukhe mṛgāḥ ||
translation: Work is accomplished by effort, not by wishes. Deer do not enter the mouth of a sleeping lion.
meaning: No amount of wishing substitutes for action. Even a lion must rise and hunt — results require effort.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: स्वभावेन हि तुष्यन्ति देवाः सत्पुरुषास्तथा । ज्ञानतुष्टाः पण्डितास्ते विद्याविनयसंयुताः ॥
transliteration: svabhāvena hi tuṣyanti devāḥ satpuruṣas tathā | jñāna-tuṣṭāḥ paṇḍitās te vidyā-vinaya-saṁyutāḥ ||
translation: Gods and noble people are satisfied by simple nature. Scholars are satisfied by knowledge — those who have both learning and humility.
meaning: Simplicity satisfies the divine; learning satisfies the scholar — humility is the common quality that elevates both.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: पुस्तकस्था तु या विद्या परहस्तगतं च धनम् । कार्यकाले समुत्पन्ने न सा विद्या न तद्धनम् ॥
transliteration: pustakasthā tu yā vidyā para-hasta-gataṁ ca dhanam | kārya-kāle samutpanne na sā vidyā na tad dhanam ||
translation: Knowledge that remains only in books and wealth in another's hands — at the time of need, neither is of any use.
meaning: Practical application is the true test of both knowledge and wealth. Internalize knowledge; hold your own wealth.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: यो ध्रुवाणि परित्यज्य ह्यध्रुवाणि निषेवते । ध्रुवाणि तस्य नश्यन्ति अध्रुवं नष्टमेव च ॥
transliteration: yo dhruvāṇi parityajya hy adhruvāṇi niṣevate | dhruvāṇi tasya naśyanti adhruvaṁ naṣṭam eva ca ||
translation: One who abandons the permanent in pursuit of the impermanent — the permanent is lost, and the impermanent perishes anyway.
meaning: Chasing the temporary at the cost of the eternal is the ultimate foolishness — you lose both.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: श्रुत्वा धर्मं विजानाति श्रुत्वा त्यजति दुर्मतिम् । श्रुत्वा ज्ञानमवाप्नोति श्रुत्वा मोक्षमवाप्नुयात् ॥
transliteration: śrutvā dharmaṁ vijānāti śrutvā tyajati durmatim | śrutvā jñānam avāpnoti śrutvā mokṣam avāpnuyāt ||
translation: By hearing one understands dharma; by hearing one abandons evil thinking; by hearing one attains knowledge; by hearing one can even attain liberation.
meaning: The act of listening — to teachers, to scripture, to wisdom — is transformative at every level, even leading to liberation.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: जलबिन्दुनिपातेन क्रमशः पूर्यते घटः । स हेतुः सर्वविद्यानां धर्मस्य च धनस्य च ॥
transliteration: jala-bindu-nipātena kramaśaḥ pūryate ghaṭaḥ | sa hetuḥ sarva-vidyānāṁ dharmasya ca dhanasya ca ||
translation: Drop by drop the pot is filled; this is the principle behind all knowledge, dharma, and wealth.
meaning: Gradual, consistent effort accumulates into mastery — there are no shortcuts.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: स्वशक्त्यनुसारेण दातव्यमेव नात्यन्तदानेन तु कीर्तिमेति । सर्वस्वदानाच्च गुणोऽधिकस्तु न सर्वस्वं देयमतिप्रसङ्गात् ॥
transliteration: sva-śakty-anusāreṇa dātavyam eva nātyanta-dānena tu kīrtim eti | sarvasva-dānāc ca guṇo'dhikas tu na sarvasvaṁ deyam ati-prasaṅgāt ||
translation: One should give according to one's capacity; giving everything does not always bring fame. Virtue is greater than giving everything away — do not give everything beyond reason.
meaning: Balanced generosity is wisdom; reckless giving of all you have is not virtue but imprudence.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अभ्यासाद्धार्यते विद्या कुलं शीलेन धार्यते । गुणेन ज्ञायते त्वार्यः कोपो नेत्रेण गम्यते ॥
transliteration: abhyāsād dhāryate vidyā kulaṁ śīlena dhāryate | guṇena jñāyate tv āryaḥ kopo netreṇa gamyate ||
translation: Knowledge is retained by practice; good family is sustained by good conduct; a noble person is known by virtue; and anger is known through the eyes.
meaning: Practice, conduct, virtue, and emotional state are all revealed through their respective indicators.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: धनहीनो न हीनश्च धनिको ह्येव पूजितः । यस्य शास्त्रं स्वकं नास्ति स दरिद्रो न संशयः ॥
transliteration: dhana-hīno na hīnaś ca dhaniko hy eva pūjitaḥ | yasya śāstraṁ svakaṁ nāsti sa daridro na saṁśayaḥ ||
translation: The poor man is not truly poor; the rich man is not truly worshiped. One who has no scriptural knowledge — that person is truly poor, without doubt.
meaning: Material poverty is not the deepest poverty — the absence of wisdom and knowledge is.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: न चोरहार्यं न च राजहार्यं न भ्रातृभाज्यं न च भारकारि । व्यये कृते वर्धते एव नित्यं विद्याधनं सर्वधनप्रधानम् ॥
transliteration: na cora-hāryaṁ na ca rāja-hāryaṁ na bhrātṛ-bhājyaṁ na ca bhārakāri | vyaye kṛte vardhate eva nityaṁ vidyā-dhanaṁ sarva-dhana-pradhānam ||
translation: It cannot be stolen by thieves, nor confiscated by kings, nor divided among brothers, nor is it heavy to carry. Spent, it grows always — the wealth of knowledge is the most important of all wealth.
meaning: Knowledge is the perfect wealth — it is indestructible, undivided, weightless, and grows with use.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सन्तोषस्त्रिषु कर्तव्यः स्वदारे भोजने धने । त्रिषु चैव न कर्तव्योऽध्ययने जपदानयोः ॥
transliteration: santoṣas triṣu kartavyaḥ sva-dāre bhojane dhane | triṣu caiva na kartavyo'dhyayane japa-dānayoḥ ||
translation: Be satisfied with your own wife, food, and wealth. But never be satisfied with your study, your japa (spiritual repetition), and your giving.
meaning: Contentment in worldly things is wisdom; but in spiritual practice and generosity, always strive for more.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: धर्मे च अर्थे च कामे च मोक्षे च भरतर्षभ । यदिहास्ति तदन्यत्र यन्नेहास्ति न तत्क्वचित् ॥
transliteration: dharme ca arthe ca kāme ca mokṣe ca bharatarṣabha | yad ihāsti tad anyatra yan nehāsti na tat kvacit ||
translation: What is here on dharma, artha, kama, and moksha — whatever is here can be found elsewhere, but what is not here is found nowhere.
meaning: This great compendium claims to contain all wisdom of the four human goals — if it is not here, it exists nowhere.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: धर्मं यं सदा पृच्छन्ति नित्यं वेदविदो जनाः । सत्यं वचनमेतेषां प्रवदन्ति पुरातनाः ॥
transliteration: dharmaṁ yaṁ sadā pṛcchanti nityaṁ veda-vido janāḥ | satyaṁ vacanam eteṣāṁ pravadanti purātanāḥ ||
translation: The knowers of the Vedas always question dharma — the ancients declare that truth is the expression of these.
meaning: The Vedic tradition is rooted in questioning — truth emerges through inquiry and honest discourse.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: राजा तु नगरे कार्यो रक्षार्थं नागरस्य च । न स्वार्थकारको राजा राजा प्रजाहिते रतः ॥
transliteration: rājā tu nagare kāryo rakṣārthaṁ nāgarasya ca | na svārtha-kārako rājā rājā prajā-hite rataḥ ||
translation: A king is established in the city for the protection of the citizens. A king who serves his own interest is no king — a true king is devoted to the welfare of the people.
meaning: Legitimate kingship is defined by service to the people, not self-interest — a ruler exists for the ruled.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: प्रजानां विनयाधानाद् रक्षणाद् भरणादपि । स राजा पार्थिवः श्रेष्ठः यः पिता इव पालयेत् ॥
transliteration: prajānāṁ vinayādhānād rakṣaṇād bharaṇād api | sa rājā pārthivaḥ śreṣṭhaḥ yaḥ pitā iva pālayet ||
translation: That king is the best ruler on earth who protects the people, provides for them, disciplines them, and governs them like a father.
meaning: The ideal ruler is a father-figure who educates, nourishes, and protects — governance as parental care.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अल्पं वा बहु वा शक्त्या दातव्यं नातिलोभिना । दानमेव हि धर्मात्मा लोके कीर्तिसुखावहम् ॥
transliteration: alpaṁ vā bahu vā śaktyā dātavyaṁ nāti-lobhinā | dānam eva hi dharmātmā loke kīrti-sukhāvaham ||
translation: Whether little or much, give according to your means, without excessive miserliness. Giving brings fame and happiness in this world for the soul of dharma.
meaning: Generosity calibrated to one's means is the mark of a dharmic soul and the source of worldly honor.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: सत्यमेव जयते नानृतं सत्येन पन्था विततो देवयानः। येनाक्रमन्त्यृषयो ह्याप्तकामा यत्र तत् सत्यस्य परमं निधानम् ॥
transliteration: satyam eva jayate nānṛtaṁ satyena panthā vitato devayānaḥ | yenākramanty ṛṣayo hy āpta-kāmā yatra tat satyasya paramaṁ nidhānam ||
translation: Truth alone conquers, not falsehood. Through truth is laid out the divine path by which the sages who have fulfilled their desires travel to where the supreme treasure of truth is.
meaning: This verse from the Mundaka Upanishad, adopted by Chanakya, declares: Truth is the supreme power and the path of the gods.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: न हि ज्ञानेन सदृशं पवित्रमिह विद्यते। तत्स्वयं योगसंसिद्धः कालेनात्मनि विन्दति ॥
transliteration: na hi jñānena sadṛśaṁ pavitram iha vidyate | tat svayaṁ yoga-saṁsiddhaḥ kālenātmani vindati ||
translation: There is nothing as purifying as knowledge in this world. One who becomes perfected in yoga finds that knowledge within themselves in time.
meaning: From the Bhagavad Gita, adopted by Chanakya: knowledge is the supreme purifier, and it is found within through yogic practice.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: अज्ञेभ्यो ग्रन्थिनः श्रेष्ठाः ग्रन्थिभ्यो धारिणो वरम् । धारिभ्यो ज्ञानिनः श्रेष्ठाः ज्ञानिभ्यो व्यवसायिनः ॥
transliteration: ajñebhyo granthinaḥ śreṣṭhāḥ granthibhyo dhāriṇo varam | dhāribhyo jñāninaḥ śreṣṭhāḥ jñānibhyo vyavasāyinaḥ ||
translation: Better than the ignorant are those who know the texts; better than those are those who remember them; better than those are those who understand them; but better than all are those who put them into practice.
meaning: The hierarchy of knowledge: memorization < understanding < application. Practical wisdom surpasses all theoretical learning.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: आयुषः क्षण एकोऽपि सर्वरत्नैर्न लभ्यते । नीयते स यमेन तु को मूढस्तं न शोचते ॥
transliteration: āyuṣaḥ kṣaṇa eko'pi sarva-ratnair na labhyate | nīyate sa yamena tu ko mūḍhas taṁ na śocate ||
translation: Even a single moment of life cannot be bought with all the gems in the world; yet it is being taken away by Yama (death). Who is so foolish as not to mourn this?
meaning: Time is the most precious and irreplaceable resource — its constant expenditure toward death should motivate us to use it wisely.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: एते सन्ति महाभागा येषां धर्मे मतिः स्थिरा। न विचलन्ति धर्माच्च शत्रुभिश्च प्रलोभिताः ॥
transliteration: ete santi mahābhāgā yeṣāṁ dharme matiḥ sthirā | na vicalanti dharmāc ca śatrubhiś ca pralobhitāḥ ||
translation: Truly fortunate are those whose minds are firm in dharma — who do not waver from righteousness even when enticed by enemies.
meaning: The greatest fortune is an unshakeable commitment to dharma that cannot be bought, bribed, or threatened away.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: दृष्टं ग्रन्थेषु यद्विज्ञं तन्निबोध वदाम्यहम् । अनुभूतं पुरुषैर्यैस्तदपि श्रुणु तत्त्वतः ॥
transliteration: dṛṣṭaṁ grantheṣu yad vijñaṁ tan nibodha vadāmy aham | anubhūtaṁ puruṣair yais tad api śṛṇu tattvataḥ ||
translation: Understand what I tell you from the scriptures; also hear truthfully what has been experienced by great persons.
meaning: Scriptural wisdom and practical experience are both valid sources of knowledge — heed both.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: परोपकारः पुण्याय पापाय परपीडनम् । इत्येतत्क्षयि विज्ञाय नैतदेव परायणम् ॥
transliteration: paropakāraḥ puṇyāya pāpāya para-pīḍanam | ity etat kṣayi vijñāya naitad eva parāyaṇam ||
translation: Helping others brings merit; harming others brings sin. Knowing this distinction — this is the ultimate teaching.
meaning: The final teaching of Chanakya is simple: the entire ethical framework reduces to this — help others, do not harm them.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: तत्त्वज्ञानमहासिन्धौ रत्नान्युत्तमतां गताः। एते सर्वे महात्मानः सारयुक्तं वदन्ति ते ॥
transliteration: tattva-jñāna-mahā-sindhau ratnāny uttamatāṁ gatāḥ | ete sarve mahātmānaḥ sāra-yuktaṁ vadanti te ||
translation: In the great ocean of the knowledge of truth, these are the gems that have attained excellence. All these great souls speak with substance.
meaning: Chanakya closes by acknowledging that these teachings are precious jewels gathered from the ocean of truth spoken by great souls.
author: Traditional

sanskrit: इत्येष चाणक्यनीतिः सुसंग्रहः कृतः। यः पठेत् स्मरेत् नित्यं स याति परमां गतिम् ॥
transliteration: ity eṣa cāṇakya-nītiḥ su-saṁgrahaḥ kṛtaḥ | yaḥ paṭhet smaret nityaṁ sa yāti paramāṁ gatim ||
translation: Thus this Chanakya Neeti is compiled as a good summary. One who reads and remembers it daily attains the highest destination.
meaning: Chanakya concludes that consistent study and remembrance of these teachings leads to the highest spiritual and worldly achievement.
author: Traditional
